currentUse: Pentecostal church — the former St. Casimir's at 41 Providence St serves a charismatic Christian congregation (Church of Pentecost); the St. Casimir facade bust with Lithuanian inscription and the Lithuanian-inscribed stained glass remain in place
summary: The monumental church at 41 Providence Street, which Worcester Lithuanians took thirteen years (1903-1916) to build, still stands and is in active religious use — sold in 2011 for $650,000 to a charismatic Pentecostal congregation (reported by the diocesan Catholic Free Press as The Church of the Pentecost, USA, Inc., with proceeds to St. John's Parish). The new owners retained the grand stained glass windows with Lithuanian inscriptions and the facade bust of St. Casimir with the Lithuanian plea "Šv. Kazimierai, melskis už mus"; the altar and other sacred items were removed before the handover. The parish itself closed officially June 30, 2008 despite an appeal by the Friends of St. Casimir, with a final Mass for Lithuanian Independence Day held in 2010. The parish's other landmark, the 1924 school building at 22 Waverly Street (facade still engraved "Šv. Kazimiero Lietuvių Mokykla"), was leased to Worcester Public Schools as the Alternative School until 2023, then sold by the diocese in December 2023 for $1.1 million with an adjacent Harrison Street parking lot; on December 18, 2024 the Worcester Planning Board approved its conversion into 32 apartments, three of them affordable. Organized Lithuanian life in the Worcester area has shifted to Maironis Park in Shrewsbury, described as the last operating Lithuanian cultural center in the area with 6-8 Lithuanian events per year; the Memorial for those who died for free Lithuania that once stood at St. Casimir's now stands there.

developments.headline: Parish officially closed despite parishioner appeal
developments.detail: Final Mass held June 28, 2008 with roughly 80 parishioners keeping vigil and handmade wooden crosses on the church lawn. The Friends of St. Casimir had submitted a formal appeal to Bishop Robert J. McManus on June 15 and planned a Vatican appeal; parishioners argued the parish was financially viable. The building remained available for funerals and special services for one additional year, and the parish merged into St. John's on Temple Street.

sources.title: Final Chimes: St. Casimir Closes Despite Protests

developments.headline: Final Mass for Lithuanian Independence Day
developments.detail: After regular liturgies ended, a last Mass was celebrated in the church in 2010 for Lithuanian independence day before the building was sold.

sources.title: Worcester, Massachusetts: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: Church building sold to Pentecostal congregation for $650,000
developments.detail: The church and attached rectory were sold to charismatic Christians for $650,000; the diocesan Catholic Free Press identified the buyer as The Church of the Pentecost, USA, Inc. (with three small house lots across Providence Street included and proceeds to St. John's Parish). Altar and sacred items were removed beforehand, but the Lithuanian-inscribed stained glass windows were fully retained and the St. Casimir bust with 'Šv. Kazimierai, melskis už mus' remains on the facade. Business directories still list Church of Pentecost USA Inc at 41 Providence St.

sources.date: undated (page returned 403; details from search excerpt)

developments.headline: Former St. Casimir's school building sold by diocese for $1.1M
developments.detail: The 1924 three-story, 27,024 sq ft school at 22 Waverly St — facade still engraved 'Šv. Kazimiero Lietuvių Mokykla' — plus an adjacent ~25-space parking lot at 54 Harrison St were sold to 22 Waverly LLC for a combined $1.1 million. The school had closed in 1986 and was leased to Worcester Public Schools as the Alternative School until June 2023; sale proceeds benefited St. John Parish.

sources.title: Century-old Worcester school building set to become apartment complex
Former site of St. Casimir's School sold by Diocese of Worcester for $1.1M

developments.headline: Planning Board approves 32-apartment conversion of the school
developments.detail: Worcester's Planning Board approved developer John D. Mulryan's plan to convert 22 Waverly St into 32 apartments (mostly one-bedrooms plus three studios, 470-800 sq ft), with three units affordable at 60% AMI and 33 parking spaces including 21 at 54 Harrison St.

sources.title: Former site of St. Casimir's School sold by Diocese of Worcester for $1.1M

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: Founding year: archive says 1898, but the Telegram & Gazette (2008-06-29) described the church as "founded 114 years" earlier (~1894), and a Catholic Free Press search excerpt says the parish was established in 1894 serving both Lithuanian and Polish communities — the 1894 vs 1898 discrepancy should be checked against parish records. Closure date: archive says July 1, 2008; the Telegram reported official closure Tuesday June 30, 2008, with the final regular Mass June 28 — an off-by-one-day refinement. Some sources (Global True Lithuania, WBJ/CFP excerpts) say the parish "closed in 2009," which reconciles with the Telegram's note that the building stayed available for funerals/special services for one more year; the archive's 2008 date is correct for the canonical closure. Financial health: archive's "solvent, debt-free" is echoed by parishioners' 2008 claims of financial viability, while the diocese cited priest shortage, suburban migration, and declining finances — a framing conflict, not a factual contradiction. Archive's note of tri-annual post-closure gatherings is consistent with the 2010 Independence Day Mass; no source found confirming those gatherings continue today. The Vytautas Ignas stained glass noted in the archive is corroborated as physically retained in the building by the new owners.
gaps: Two key press pages returned HTTP 403 and could not be opened directly — the Catholic Free Press sale article (buyer's corporate name "The Church of the Pentecost, USA, Inc.", the three house lots, and proceeds-to-St.-John's detail come from its search excerpt, corroborated in substance by the opened Global True Lithuania page) and the Worcester Business Journal school-sale article (its $1.1M figure and 22 Waverly LLC buyer are corroborated by the opened Telegram & Gazette/Yahoo article). Exact closing date and deed details of the 2011 church sale were not verified against registry records. No 2025-2026 press about the church building, no evidence found of current organized St. Casimir reunions or preservation campaigns (searches for reunion/Maironis Park events 2024-2025 returned nothing specific), and the current physical condition of the Vytautas Ignas windows is known only from Global True Lithuania's statement that they were retained. Whether the apartment conversion of the school has begun construction as of mid-2026 was not found.
